summary:
The Caliphate of Cordoba (929–1031 AD) was the most sophisticated and powerful state in medieval Europe — the period when Abd al-Rahman III declared himself caliph (rather than merely emir), raising al-Andalus to equal status with the [[event:2133|Abbasid Caliphate]] in Baghdad and the [[event:2194|Fatimid Caliphate]] in Cairo. At its peak, the Cordoba Caliphate controlled most of the Iberian Peninsula and parts of North Africa, and its capital Cordoba was the largest city in Europe with an estimated population of 500,000 — far surpassing anything in northern Europe.

Cordoba under the caliphate was the intellectual capital of Europe — a city of reportedly 70 libraries (the largest containing 400,000 volumes), hundreds of mosques, paved streets (when Paris had none), street lighting, and a sophisticated sewer system. The caliphate was the meeting point where Greek learning (preserved and extended by Arab scholars) was available to European scholars, making Cordoba a center for the transmission of scientific, philosophical, and mathematical knowledge from the Islamic to the Christian world. Jewish scholars (Moses ibn Ezra, Maimonides's predecessors) flourished in relative tolerance. The [[event:2193|Great Mosque of Cordoba]] (the Mezquita) was its architectural monument. The caliphate collapsed in civil war (the Fitna) 1009–1031 AD, fragmenting into small successor states (taifas) that collectively weakened Muslim Iberia and facilitated the Christian [[event:2209|Reconquista]].
